raw_branches.text: OKRs are framed as abstract outcome targets without concrete, event-based trigger-action cues to initiate the tracking and execution behavior.
raw_branches.cite: Gollwitzer (1999) 'Implementation Intentions' and habit trigger-action cue research

raw_branches.text: Weekly tracking processes require manual high-friction reflection, which fails when the tracking behavior itself is not established as a default automatic routine.
raw_branches.cite: BJ Fogg 'Tiny Habits' behavior design (Behavior = Motivation + Ability + Prompt)

raw_branches.text: OKRs rely on delayed, long-term outcome rewards rather than immediate intrinsic rewards, failing to neurologically reinforce the habit of OKR review.
raw_branches.cite: Charles Duhigg 'The Power of Habit' habit loop (Cue, Routine, Reward)

raw_branches.text: Personal OKRs lack external social accountability and reputational stakes, meaning there is no negative consequence or social cost for abandoning them.
raw_branches.cite: Behavioral economics literature on commitment devices and social accountability

raw_branches.text: Personal OKRs fail to align long-term goals with immediate rewards, leading to temporal discounting where short-term incentives consistently override the delayed benefits of OKR completion.
raw_branches.cite: Temporal discounting and reward incentive structures in behavioral psychology

raw_branches.text: The emotional incentive is heavily front-loaded in the goal-setting phase, meaning users satisfy their motivation by planning without any structural incentive to sustain the effort of execution.
raw_branches.cite: Goal-setting theory and dopamine reward dynamics in behavior change

raw_branches.text: Goals are framed as lagging outcomes rather than weekly behaviors, leaving users with no measurable progress to track in the first few weeks.
OKR tracking requires manual high-friction updates that exhaust willpower.
raw_branches.cite: General knowledge on goal tracking overhead and measurement complexity

raw_branches.text: No recurring review ritual is scheduled on the calendar, causing the habit of measurement to silently drift and be forgotten.

deduped_branches.text: OKRs are framed as abstract lagging outcomes without concrete trigger-action cues, leaving users with no measurable progress to track in the first few weeks.
The emotional incentive is heavily front-loaded in the goal-setting phase because the act of planning simulates goal completion and releases dopamine, satisfying motivation before execution begins.
deduped_branches.cite: Goal-setting theory and dopamine reward dynamics

deduped_branches.text: Weekly tracking processes require manual high-friction reflection and updates that exhaust willpower because the tracking behavior is not integrated into existing daily workflows.
deduped_branches.cite: BJ Fogg 'Tiny Habits' behavior design & Goal tracking UX research

deduped_branches.text: Personal OKRs lack external social accountability and reputational stakes because they are kept private, meaning there is no negative consequence or social cost for abandoning them.

Personal OKRs rely on delayed outcome rewards and fail to align long-term goals with immediate incentives, leading to temporal discounting where short-term demands consistently override delayed benefits.
deduped_branches.cite: Charles Duhigg 'The Power of Habit' & Temporal discounting and reward structures

deduped_branches.text: No recurring review ritual is scheduled on the calendar because goal reviews are treated as ad-hoc, low-priority tasks rather than non-negotiable calendar commitments.

load_bearing_sent_to_refute.text: Willpower exhaustion and tracking tool complexity are the primary bottlenecks to OKR completion.
load_bearing_sent_to_refute.cite: General tracking tools overhead and willpower assertions

refutations.evidence: Users fail even when using simple text files/spreadsheets or high willpower, indicating a process/habit structure constraint, not tool overhead or effort.
